title: RPYR-0013 — `tuple.__hash__` recurses without a guard: hashing a deeply nested tuple (even just using it as a dict key) overflows the native stack → SIGSEGV (crates/vm/src/builtins/tuple.rs:683)
found_by: recursion-guard-auditor (the same-file slot asymmetry: `repr` enters a ReprGuard at tuple.rs:535, `hash` enters no recursion guard)
CPython differential / shared-crash ledger (both interpreters SIGSEGV; tracker check found it unreported → filed upstream as CPython #154318)
toolkit_class: unguarded-protocol-recursion (native C/Rust stack overflow, NOT a catchable RecursionError)
cpython_behavior: identical — CPython also SIGSEGVs, in `tuple_hash` (Objects/tupleobject.c:385, unguarded `PyObject_Hash(item[i])`). Its newly added `frozendict` (main only) carries the same defect via copied code (`frozendict_pair_hash`). Filed upstream, both sites, as #154318. This is a SHARED-BUG, not ACCEPTABLE-by-contract.
guarded_twin: In the SAME file: `Representable::repr` (tuple.rs:532) enters `ReprGuard` (tuple.rs:535) and comparison is depth-guarded, so `repr(t)` and `t == t` both degrade to a clean `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth` at depth 300k — only `Hashable::hash` (tuple.rs:499 → `tuple_hash` :682 → `val.hash(vm)` :683) enters NO recursion guard and SIGSEGVs. Byte-for-byte the same asymmetry CPython has (comparison/repr guarded, hash not).
affected: using a deeply nested tuple as a dict key / set member / frozenset element (insertion hashes it)
types.GenericAlias.__hash__ (hashes its __args__ tuple, so it dies in tuple_hash)
prior_art: Shared with CPython — filed as CPython #154318 (this session). RustPython umbrella #2796 (= fuzzer RUSTPY-0007a). Same defect CLASS as the genericalias `make_parameters` recursion (CPython #154275; recorded RPYR-0015), a distinct site (element hash vs parameter walk). The SYSTEMIC RustPython root/fix is RPYR-0014 (the hash dispatch at protocol/object.rs:695 is unguarded) — RPYR-0014 supersedes this entry's per-slot tuple.rs fix.
notes: RustPython `tuple_hash` (tuple.rs:682) maps `val.hash(vm)` over the elements (`hash::hash_tuple(elements.iter().map(|val| val.hash(vm)))`, tuple.rs:683) with no `vm.with_recursion(...)`. A nested tuple recurses one native frame per level to the full nesting depth and overflows the stack (SIGSEGV) instead of raising RecursionError. Threshold is stack-size dependent (~200-300k on an 8 MB stack). The crash needs no explicit `hash()`: `{t: 1}`, `{t}`, `frozenset({t})` all hash the key on insertion and SIGSEGV the same way. FIX: wrap the element hashing in `vm.with_recursion("while hashing", || ...)` — the depth guard `repr`/`compare` already use — NOT `ReprGuard`, which keys on object-id re-entrance (for cycles) and would not catch deep *acyclic* nesting. frozenset is immune in both interpreters (elements are hashed+cached at construction, so the top-level hash is O(1)); RustPython has no `frozendict`, so the CPython issue's second site does not apply here.
